entry.title: Re: WM Inbound Putaway Public Cloud
entry.content:
Good day @afiqhazmi_mohdrazmi7186af 

Thank you for your question.

For the scenario you described the setup i recommend is for you to use HU based putaway. If 3 pallets arrive, each physical pallet should be represented as a separate HU, so that the system can process each pallet individually instead of trying to split one large product quantity during putaway.

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/e4e0a2dbf30a417c9f3d67724608a04f.html?locale=en-US

Question 1: How does the system handle multiple pallets when “Do Not Split Putaway” is active?

Answer: You should understand “Do Not Split Putaway” as a control to prevent one putaway task quantity from being split across multiple bins. If the system receives one unpacked quantity of 3000 kg and the bin capacity is 1000 kg, the system should not automatically treat this as 3 separate pallets. The proper design is for you to create 3 pallet Hus, each with its own HU number and pallet quantity.

Question 2: What is the best way to handle this scenario properly — should we use Handling Unit (HU) Management so that each pallet is processed individually?

Answer: What the system requires as a setup is for you to maintain the pallet packaging material and HU type, allow the HU type in the destination storage type or bin type, and maintain bin capacity such as maximum weight or volume.

Here are references for more guidance

3535831 - HU dimension(Height, length or width) is not check during capacity check - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/8a3d957636c245ab9e4de3c2285888b7.html?locale=en-US

Question 3: Are there recommended configurations (e.g., HU type, storage type settings, capacity check) to ensure each pallet is put away correctly without violating the “Do Not Split Putaway” rule?

Answer: As a next step, test the process by creating an inbound delivery with 3 separate pallet Hus, creating putaway warehouse tasks and confirming in Warehouse Monitor that each HU receives its own valid putaway destination. If product warehouse tasks are created instead of HU warehouse tasks, review the warehouse process type and related configuration.

Here are references for more guidance

3575256 - Product Warehouse Tasks are being created instead of HU Warehouse Tasks for inbound/outbound delivery - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Changing Serial Number
entry.content:
Dear @Geerthana_Mohanaranjan,

The serial number record can only be updated within the scope of the released standard functionality. SAP provides the released Material Serial Number API API_MATERIALSERIALNUMBER, which supports creating, reading and updating serial numbers for a material. However this update capability should not be interpreted as a way to freely overwrite the serial number assignment, stock status or logistics history after an incorrect goods movement, delivery, production confirmation or other logistics posting has already been completed

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/9ce855c709ce4f5c99b685903461106e/bf7dd26ebaaa49f3beaa3ddb8977b728.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f9ba73d1b8c543c2bd2ba1666271af86/b6116dbbcff74afd851343ca65c54d3c.html

For stock-related serial number data, SAP confirms the Public Cloud design very explicitly fields such as Plant, Storage Location and Sales Order are only filled while the serial number is in warehouse stock with status ESTO. When the serial number leaves stock, these fields are cleared by the system and in status ESTO the fields cannot be edited directly. The correct reference for historical assignments is the serial number history, not a manual overwrite of these fields

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2980219

Therefore, if the requirement is to correct a serial number that was used incorrectly in a posted logistics process the standard approach is to correct the underlying business document flow where possible for example by reversing/cancelling the incorrect posting and reposting the transaction with the correct serial number. For integration scenarios the correct design is to pass the correct serial number during the relevant goods movement. The Public Cloud Material Documents API supports material document read/create scenarios and includes serial number information for material document processing

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3f57e7df4a114edabffe8b2d581a59ed/d4c919581bc30a02e10000000a44147b.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3f57e7df4a114edabffe8b2d581a59ed/842c673978bd46d4874e8b9121743b70.html

For future control the process should be secured through the standard serial number profile setup and process design so the correct serial number is entered or passed at the time of posting rather than corrected afterwards. SAP standard serial number handling is driven by serial number profiles assigned to the material

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/900eb753128eb44ce10000000a174cb4.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: When running report /SDF/RC_START_CHECK, phase CT_CHK_ITEM_DEVICE fails with 'return code = 7'
entry.content:
As Support Engineer, I have seen that many customers are reporting issues during the simplification check report execution - /SDF/RC_START_CHECK.
In phase CT_CHK_ITEM_DEVICE, which checks for invalidated output devices, system returns 'return code = 7' for some specific output devices.
Then, customers are not sure why this occurs and which actions need to be taken.

In this blog post, I will explain why this occurs and how to solve this.

During phase CT_CHK_ITEM_DEVICE, access methods assigned to output devices are checked.
In S/4HANA Output Control OP2021 and higher, access methods "G", "F" and "M" are not supported.

Only the following access methods listed in SAP Note 0003057330 - "Simplification Item SI28: CT_CHK_ITEM_DEVICE - Check for invalidated Output Devices" are supported:

C - Direct operating system call
E - External output management system
L - Print locally using LP/LPR
S - Print using SAP protocol
U - Print using Berkeley protocol

The reason for this is when S/4HANA Output Control frame issues output for a document, it is done using a Background Remote Function call, which does not support front end printing. 

If a printer is assigned to an output request item for a document which is not assigned to one of the supported access methods, the output type will not be issued, instead it will remain in status "In Preparation". 
This was not a restriction in the past releases, however this restriction is now being enforced as of OP2021 because Frontend printing has never been supported in S/4HANA Output Control.

Therefore, customers have to customize the output devices in transaction SPAD to one of the supported access methods listed in note 0003057330.

Also, the same topic is addressed in note 0003238580:

...
4) Frontend printing is not supported by SAP S/4HANA Output Control framework (Access Methods: F - Printing On Frontend Computer / G - Frontend Printing With Control Tech.). 
... 

 
Therefore, in SPAD, for the affected output device, access method should be customized to one of the supported listed above.

This setting will help to pass phase CT_CHK_ITEM_DEVICE and execute report /SDF/RC_START_CHECK successfully.

entry.title: Has anyone replicated CKM3N logic using direct SAP database tables?
entry.content:
Hello everyone,
I am working on a BI/DW requirement where we need to replicate, or at least understand, the logic behind transaction CKM3N using data extracted directly from the SAP database.
I know that CKM3N is not based on a simple query. It seems to be built using ABAP logic, Dynpro screens, internal structures, ALV Tree components, and Material Ledger calculations.
 
So far, I have identified some technical objects such as:

 - Transaction: CKM3N

- ALV component/program: CL_ALV_TREE_BASE===============CP
- Display structure: FCML4H_LIST_S
- Table identified through technical information: MLDOC
- Field example: MLDOC-CATEG

 My main goal is not only to identify the tables, but to understand how the CKM3N output is constructed, including:

 

- Main source tables used
- Relationships between the tables
- Calculation logic for quantities, values, prices, and categories
- How the ALV Tree output is built
- Whether there is any standard function module, class, CDS view, or API that can be reused instead of rebuilding everything manually
 
Has anyone already replicated CKM3N data or logic for reporting purposes outside SAP, such as in a BI/DW environment?

Any guidance, technical references, tables, function modules, classes, or recommended approach would be very helpful.

 

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Troubleshooting the NACE/NAST Output Determination in Inventory Management
entry.content:
The BRF+ output determination framework is available for Inventory Management in S/4HANA. However, the traditional NACE/NAST output determination process still fully works and can be used in the same way as in ECC systems. When troubleshooting issues related to this output determination technology, SAP Note 426554 is an essential reference for analysis.

This blog is divided into two parts. The first part demonstrates, through a practical example, how to review and complete Checklist 1 from SAP Note 426554 to ensure that all required configurations are correctly maintained. The second part provides a detailed explanation of the system process flow, including debugging points that can help troubleshoot issues related to the classic output determination process.

Image/data in this Blog Post is from SAP internal systems, sample data, or demo systems. Any resemblance to real data is purely coincidental.

⠀

Customizing checklist #1 from SAP Note 426554

The example used here was executed on an old SAP ERP system running SAP_APPL package level 605. It consists of an initial entry of stock post on MIGO transaction using movement type 561 with an individual slip for output type WE01.

The MM-IM customizing can be reached using SPRO transaction, IMG path: 'Materials Management' → 'Inventory Management and Physical Inventory', but for short, transaction OLMB will be used to access the subfolders of that path.

1. Output type to be generated

For this example we want to use a WE01 output type with Individual Slip.

2. Form and Print program

The Form, Print Program and Form Routine assigned to the output type to be used can be checked on path OMLB → 'Output Determination' → 'Assign Forms and Programs':

  • Program: SAPM07DR
  • Form Routine: ENTRY_WE01
  • Form: WESCHEINVERS1

Transaction M706 or transaction NACE + Application 'ME', selecting the output type and then clicking on 'Processing Routines' will show the same result and can also be used as alternate paths for further information such as access sequences, condition tables, condition record key fields, etc.

3. Access Sequence and Condition Table

The standard Access Sequence range is from 0001 to 0007 and can be consulted using path OMLB → 'Output Determination'  → 'Maintain Output Types'  or transaction M706 and then double-click on the output type that you wish to check, in this case output type WE01:

Double-click on the Access Sequence number will lead us to a screen where double-clicking the folder 'Accesses' will show us our condition table (standard range from 070 to 075):

Inventory management uses B tables for the condition records, so table 72 corresponds to database table B072.

The subfolder 'Fields' corresponds to the key fields that will be used on the condition table (item 6.) and will be mapped into the communication structures KOMKBME and KOMPBME:

  • Access Sequence: 0003
  • Condition table: 72 (B072 in SE16)
  • Key fields:
    • VGART - KOMKBME - Trans./Event Type
    • WEVER - KOMKBME - Print Version
    • KZDRU - KOMPBME - Print item

4. Transaction used

We are using transaction MIGO to post the 561, on the MIGO screen we are selecting the print version 1 for the 'Individual Slip' (according to the F1 - Help for the field in MIGO):

The operation 'Goods Receipt' + 'Others' with movement type 561 assigns the transaction code 'MIGO_GI' for the header of the material document and in table T158, the transaction event type 'WA' is assigned to that transaction.

  • Transaction: MIGO_GI
  • Print Version (WEVER): 1 (Individual Slip)
  • Transaction Event Type (VGART): WA

5. Movement Type

Under the path OLMB → 'Print Control' → 'Maintain Print Indicator for Goods Receipt Documents' we can see the print item (KZDRU) assigned to the movement type 561:

This indicator can also be checked on OMJJ transaction:

  • Movement type: 561
  • Print Item (KZDRU): 1

6. Condition Record key fields

On Item 3. we found the key fields for the condition record select:

  • VGART - KOMKBME - Trans./Event Type
  • WEVER - KOMKBME - Print Version
  • KZDRU - KOMPBME - Print item

On Itens 4. and 5. we found the values for those fields:

  • VGART - KOMKBME - Trans./Event Type = WA
  • WEVER - KOMKBME - Print Version = 1
  • KZDRU - KOMPBME - Print item = 1

Now we need to check if there is a Condition Record for those values based on the Condition Table. To do this, we can use transaction NACE or path OLMB → 'Output Determination' → 'Maintain Conditions' → 'Display Condition: Inventory Mgmt' with the output type WE01:

If a condition record is found, the information we need from it is the Transmission time-spot (VSZTP) or Dispatch Time under the field 'Date/Time'.

Alternatively, this information can be found using the B table for the condition number found and then using the condition record number (KNUMH) in table NACH.

  • Transmission time-spot of the condition record (VSZTP) = 4 (Send immediately (when saving the application)).

7. Print Indicator

For the print indicator, since we are posting the movement on MIGO, the flag is marked on the header:

8. Output Record

To check the output record status, we have to open the material document posted in MIGO using the operation 'Display' → 'Material Document'  and then click on 'Display outputs':

9. Printer

When displaying the output created in MIGO transaction, you can check the communication method for the printer selected:

Debugging

1. Form DRUCK_PRUEFEN (MM07MFD0)

In this form, we check the RM07M structure for the Print Indicator (XNAPR) to confirm if  the Print Version set on MIGO (WVERS1, WVERS2 or WVERS3) needs to be saved on MKPF.  The version is updated on MKPF-WEVER field.

  IF NOT RM07M-WVERS1 IS INITIAL. 
    MKPF-WEVER = 1.
    T158-WEVER = 1.
  ENDIF.
  IF NOT RM07M-WVERS2 IS INITIAL.
    MKPF-WEVER = 2.
    T158-WEVER = 2.
  ENDIF.
[...]

In this form we also set the XNAPR flag based on the NDR parameter of the user in case the goods movement was posted via BAPI/Idoc:

* Nachlesen Parameter NDR, wenn Verarbeitung über MB_CREATE -----------*
  IF NOT XMBWL IS INITIAL.
    IF NOT PR_UNAME IS INITIAL.
      PRINT_UNAME = PR_UNAME.
    ENDIF.
    IF PR_PRINT IS INITIAL.
      SELECT SINGLE * FROM USR05 WHERE BNAME = PRINT_UNAME
                                 AND   PARID = 'NDR'.
      IF SY-SUBRC IS INITIAL.
        RM07M-XNAPR = USR05-PARVA.
      ELSE.
        CLEAR RM07M-XNAPR.
      ENDIF.
    ELSEIF PR_PRINT = 1.
      RM07M-XNAPR = X.
    ELSEIF PR_PRINT = 2.
      CLEAR RM07M-XNAPR.
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.
*---------

If the Print Indicator is not set, we clear the default print version so the system don't find any condition, not generating any slip. But the whole process for its determination is executed. It's not possible to skip the output determination, only to make sure it will not yield any results if not needed.

  IF RM07M-XNAPR IS INITIAL.
    CLEAR T158-WEVER.
  ENDIF.
ENDFORM.

For the example we are running, RM07M-XNAPR is set to X since we marked the flag in MIGO and the version is defined as 1 for both MKPF-WEVER and T158-WEVER because RM07M-WVERS1 is X, corresponding to version 1 (Individual Slip).

2. Form BUCHEN_NUMMERNVERGABE (MM07MFB9)

Here's where the call for the forms KOPFDIALOGSTRUKTUR_FUELLEN, POSIDIALOGSTRUKTUR_FUELLEN and MESSAGING occurs to build the communication structure for the header, item and then trigger the actual output determination, respectively:

 PERFORM kopfdialogstruktur_fuellen USING space. "<<< Build KOMKBME 

  LOOP AT xmseg.
    xindex = sy-tabix.
    xmseg-mblnr = xmkpf-mblnr.
    IF xmseg-lgpla IS INITIAL AND NOT xmseg-xblvs IS INITIAL.
      xmseg-lgpla = xmkpf-mblnr.
    ENDIF.
    IF xmseg-lfbnr IS INITIAL AND ( xmseg-kzbew = b OR t158-kzbew = b ).
      LOOP AT lfbnr WHERE zeile = xmseg-zeile.
        xmseg-lfbnr = xmkpf-mblnr.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.
    MODIFY xmseg INDEX xindex.
    READ TABLE xvm07m INDEX xmseg-zeile.
    PERFORM posidialogstruktur_fuellen USING space. "<<< Build KOMPBME
    PERFORM messaging USING space. "<<< Output determination

3. Form KOPFDIALOGSTRUKTUR_FUELLEN (MM07MFN0)

Here we have the call to the function COMMUNICATION_AREA_KOMKBME from SD area to fill the communication structure KOMKMBME with the information from the header of the material document (MKPF) like the Transaction Event Type (MKPF-VGART) and the Print Version (MKPF-WEVER).

This structure is used along with KOMPBME to define the condition table based on the access sequence on SD_COND_ACCESS function later in the process.

  CALL FUNCTION 'COMMUNICATION_AREA_KOMKBME'
       EXPORTING
            MSG_MKPF     = NMKPF
            MSG_T158     = T158
            MSG_PR_UNAME = PRINT_UNAME
       TABLES
            TAB_PART = PART.

Inside the function we can check the data returned to the imported structure KOMKBME:

4. Form POSIDIALOGSTRUKTUR_FUELLEN (MM07MFN0)

Like the previous form, we call the function COMMUNICATION_AREA_KOMPBME to fill the communication structure KOMPBME with the information of the items for the material document (MSEG) like the Print Item (T156-KZDRU) from Movement Type (MSEG-BWART), Plant (MSEG-WERKS), Storage Location (MSEG-LGORT), etc.

CALL FUNCTION 'COMMUNICATION_AREA_KOMPBME'
    EXPORTING
        MSG_MSEG   = NMSEG
        MSG_T156   = T156
        MSG_VM07M  = NVM07M
        MSG_MKPF   = MKPF
    TABLES
        TAB_PART = PART.

Inside of it we can check also check the return on structure KOMPBME:

5. Form MESSAGING (MM07MFN0)

This is where the actual output determination occurs based on all the information gathered previously and stored in KOMKBME and KOMPBME structures.

The condition technique from SD area is used for the determination of the condition table based on the access sequence determined by the key fields of the selected output.

The function MESSAGING starts the process by calling Form Routine COND_ACCESS_ME (LV61BMES) since the NACE application for the inventory management output is ME.

* do condition access if form already exists
    PERFORM (L_CONDITION_FORM) IN PROGRAM SAPLV61B USING APPLIKATION
                                                        SCHEMA
                                                    IF FOUND.
* L_CONDITION_FORM = COND_ACCESS_ME

6. Form COND_ACCESS_ME (LV61BMES)

The internal tables KOMT1 e KOMT2 are filled in Form KONDITIONSVORSTEP(SAPLV61B).

KOMT1 will be filled with the output types configured for Inventory Management (KAPPL = 'ME'). The column KOZGF corresponds to the access sequence linked to the output types in column KSCHL:

KOMT2 will be filled with the condition tables for each access sequence. The correspondence in MM-IM is 1:1.

When both tables are filled, we start a nested loop on them by calling the forms XNAST_AUFBAUEN_AUS_KOMT1 (LV61BF0X) and NACHRICHTENSTATUS_SCHREIBEN (LV61BF0N).

7. Form NACHRICHTENSTATUS_SCHREIBEN (LV61BF0N)

In this form we execute the call to the condition technique function SD_COND_ACCESS:

*    start condition technique - find message type
        CALL FUNCTION 'SD_COND_ACCESS'
        EXPORTING
            application                = komt1-kappl
            condition_type             = komt1-kschl
            header_comm_area           = header_comm
            position_comm_area         = item_comm
            prestep                    = ' '
            protocol_access            = protocol_access
            read_only_one_record       = 'X'
            t682i_i                    = t682i
            koprt_i                    = koprt
            sdprothead_i               = g_sdprothead
        IMPORTING
            condition_is_purely_header = headkz
        TABLES
            condition_records          = kondtab
        EXCEPTIONS
            field_is_initial           = 8
            not_read_unqualified       = 2
            read_but_not_found         = 4.

8. Function SD_COND_ACCESS

Inside this function, we will run the form SEL_KONDTAB:

* do access
    PERFORM sel_kondtab TABLES condition_records
                        USING  t682i_i
                                application
                                condition_type
                                date
                                prestep
                                read_only_one_record
                                protocol_access
                                protocol_date
                                sdprothead_i
                                koprt_i
                                condition_in_memory_internal
                                call_modus
                                read_all_prestep
                    CHANGING condition_is_purely_header
                                condition_is_in_memory.

On that form, we loop through the key fields of the access sequence to check if they are filled. With that information, we perform a dynamic select on the condition table inside T681-KOTAB together with the key field values inside CODING_TAB internal table.

Since the select is dynamic, we do not know the exact point where it will be executed in SEL_KONDTAB form, so in order to check it we can place a breakpoint in the statement below and go from there:

IF se_memory_internal EQ no OR se_t682i-gzugr NE 'B'. "<<< Breakpoint here
    h_subrc = 4.
    IF se_prestep              = yes OR
        se_read_only_one_record = yes.
        IF t681-ksdat IS INITIAL.
        IF se_read_all_prestep IS INITIAL.
            SELECT * FROM (t681-kotab) APPENDING TABLE <cond_tab>
                    UP TO 1 ROWS
                    WHERE kappl  = se_kappl
                    AND   kschl  = se_kschl
                    AND   (coding_tab).
            h_subrc = sy-subrc.
        ELSE.
            SELECT * FROM (t681-kotab) APPENDING TABLE <cond_tab>
                    WHERE kappl  = se_kappl
                    AND   kschl  = se_kschl
                    AND   (coding_tab).
        ENDIF.

The statement below in form NACHRICHTENSTATUS_SCHREIBEN checks if the condition record number (XKNUMH) was found by the SEL_KONDTAB select (sy-subrc = 0 returned):

IF xknumh NE space.
     *nach-kschl = space.
    xsubrc = 1.
    READ TABLE xnach WITH KEY key-knumh = xknumh BINARY SEARCH.
    IF sy-subrc NE 0.

If the system found the record number, an entry is added to internal table XNAST.

9. Form BUCHEN_AUSFUEHREN (MM07MFB9)

In this form we can check XNAST table to confirm the entries built into it before the system starts the update tasks.

10.  Form PROGRAMM_AUFRUFEN (RSNAST00) (UPDATE MODE)

It is not possible to debug the print program SAPM07DR because the same is executed in update task by the call below in Form PROGRAMM_AUFRUFEN (RSNAST00), also executed in update task:

perform (tnapr-ronam) in program (tnapr-pgnam) using returncode
                                                     us_screen
                                                     if found.

For our example, TNAPR-RONAM = ENTRY_WE01 and TNAPR-PGNAM = SAPM07DR.

Those are the main points of the old output determination process run by Inventory Management, if the output is not triggered for some reason when the document is posted, those points can be useful to find out what information was missing and adjust the customizing accordingly.

⠀

Further Information

  • 426554 - Output determination checklist for goods movements
  • Output Determination in Inventory Management (IM)
  • Troubleshooting the New Output Management in Inventory Management

entry.title: Re: Data preparation in Project Billing in SAP S/4HANA Pu...
entry.content:
Good day @varunjain134 

Thank you for your question.

Question 1: For Time and Material Billing, can we add "service rendered date" as a aggregation attribute? We need separate line items in the billing document request based on the service rendered date

Answer: No, there is no standard configuration in Manage Project Billing or customization/CBC where you can add Service Rendered Date as an additional aggregation attribute to force separate Billing Document Request items by date.

For Time and Expenses billing, the service rendered date is available in the project billing process and is also part of the project billing request item data.

However, the aggregation into the Billing Document Request is controlled by the standard project billing data preparation and pricing relevant attributes.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/4341542a121d4c87a0b05c2fa5a4a709.html?locale=en-US

3355211 - Difference of To Bill value in Project Billing Request and Net Value of Billing Document Request. - SAP for Me

So, in simple terms:

  • The service rendered date exists in the project billing data.
  • It can be used for billing and tax related processing.
  • But it is not exposed as a configurable aggregation key in standard project billing.
  • You cannot add it as a custom aggregation attribute to split BDR line items by service rendered date.
  • If your business requires this exact line split in the BDR, you will need to raise this as a feature request. See KBA 2963059 on how to go about that.

Question 2: In the Project Billing Request, can we have pricing simulation for fixed price and periodic billing ?

Answer: Not in the same way as a free pricing simulation screen inside the Project Billing Request.

For Fixed Price and Periodic Services, the billable values are driven mainly by the billing plans items maintained on the customer project billing item. Note that fixed price billing uses billing plan items with amount and billing due data, while periodic service billing uses billing plan items with amount, billing due date, and period of service.

The Manage Project Billing app lets the billing specialist prepare billing, include or exclude billing plan items, and submit the Project Billing Request to generate the Billing Document Request. Note that Manage Project Billing supports Fixed Price, Time and Expenses, Periodic Service, and Usage Based billing elements.

For recalculation of billing amounts, the relevant app is Schedule Repricing for Projects. You can use this app is used to update billing amounts when sales rates change, material configuration changes, or the To Be Billed amount does not reflect correctly in Manage Project Billing.

See the following references for more details.

https://learning.sap.com/courses/exploring-sales-intercompany-processes-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-professional-services/understanding-process-steps-and-billing-documents-for-project-billing

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/29cf986299714386847f4d4c9bb86994/09bddf5612e54600a06539c2e4250d30.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/29cf986299714386847f4d4c9bb86994/1e8266b9efe1478b93818bb5f14b7072.html?locale=en-US

So, the practical answer is:

  • For Fixed Price and Periodic Services, the amount comes from the billing plan setup.
  • You can review the amount in Manage Project Billing before you submit it.
  • You can use Schedule Repricing for Projects where repricing is relevant.
  • You can preview the invoice after the Billing Document Request is created, where supported.
  • But there is no separate pricing simulation function inside the Project Billing Request editor for fixed price and periodic billing like a standalone simulation button.

Here is the approach i recommend:

For the first requirement, if the purpose is only to make the customer invoice easier to read by service date, check whether the service date is available in the output form or item text. If the requirement is that the BDR must contain separate item lines by service rendered date, this is not available as a standard aggregation configuration.

For you second requirement, use the standard project billing flow:

  1. Maintain the billing plan correctly in the customer project.
  2. Use Manage Project Billing to review the fixed price or periodic service billing item.                                                  
  3. Use Schedule Repricing for Projects if the amount is not updated correctly of if a rate/material change requires recalculation.                                                                                                                               
  4. Submit the Project Billing Request.
  5. Review the generated Billing Document Request or preliminary billing document before final billing.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Deploying a UI5 Library to Application Frontend Service
entry.content:
Background

In a previous blog, I introduced an approach to simplify the use of a custom UI5 library on SAP BTP: bundling the library directly into the consuming application at build time. This eliminates the need for destination-related configuration in the consuming application, which is otherwise required when the library is deployed independently to HTML5 Application Repository.

However, this approach has a drawback: whenever the library is updated, any consuming application that needs to pick up the change must be rebuilt and redeployed individually.

Recently, I came across the blog UI Composition with Application Frontend Service by @VadimTomnikov, which introduced a more elegant solution. When using Application Frontend Service, a consuming application can reference a separately deployed library simply by adding a route in its xs-app.json. No destination configuration is needed, and the library version can even be specified per application.

This blog describes how to deploy a UI5 library to Application Frontend Service and consume it from another application.

Note: Application Frontend Service is not a replacement for HTML5 Application Repository — the two services currently target different use cases. In particular, native Work Zone integration is not yet supported; applications can be accessed via a direct URL or published as a Business Solution, but cannot be onboarded through the standard channel-based integration. If your use case requires tight Work Zone integration, HTML5 Application Repository remains the appropriate choice.

Prerequisites

  • Basic understanding of Application Frontend Service. If you are new to it, refer to Introducing Application Frontend Service.
  • Application Frontend Service is subscribed in your BTP subaccount. For setup instructions, refer to this blog post.
  • A UI5 library and a consuming application. This blog uses the same library and application created in the previous blog. Refer to Steps 1 and 2 of that blog for setup instructions.

Steps Overview

  1. Configure the Consuming Application
  2. Configure the Library for Deployment to Application Frontend Service
  3. Deploy and Verify

1. Configure the Consuming Application

1.1 Generate Deployment Configuration

Generate the deployment configuration for the consuming application using the SAP Fiori tools. When prompted, select Cloud Foundry as the target and Add Application to Application Frontend Service as the Router Module.

Figure: Deployment Configuration dialog — select Application Frontend Service as the Router Module.

1.2 xs-app.json

Add a route for the library before the other routes. The application property references the deployed library by its ID, optionally followed by @ and the version.

{
  "welcomeFile": "/index.html",
  "authenticationMethod": "route",
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/resources/com/myorg/reuselib/(.*)$",
      "target": "/$1",
      "application": "com.myorg.reuselib@1.0.0"
    },
    ...
  ]
}

2. Configure the Library for Deployment to Application Frontend Service

When a UI5 library is generated using the "Fiori: Open Reusable Library Generator", the generated package.json includes a deploy-config script that runs fiori add deploy-config. However, this command does not support generating Cloud Foundry / Application Frontend deployment configuration for library projects — only ABAP targets are available. Therefore, the following files must be created manually.

Files to Create or Update

TypeFileDescription
Createmta.yamlMTA module and resources for Application Frontend and XSUAA
Createui5-deploy.yamlUI5 build configuration with ui5-task-zipper
Createxs-app.jsonRouting configuration for Application Frontend
Createxs-security.jsonXSUAA configuration
Updatepackage.jsonAdd build and deploy scripts, and ui5-task-zipper to devDependencies

2.1 ui5-deploy.yaml

Create this file based on the consuming application's ui5-deploy.yaml. When adapting it for a library project, two adjustments are required.

Set afterTask to buildThemes in ui5-task-zipper

The generateCachebusterInfo task used in application builds does not run for libraries. The last task in a library build is buildThemes. Set afterTask to buildThemes as shown below.

- name: ui5-task-zipper
  afterTask: buildThemes
  configuration:
    archiveName: commyorgreuselib
    relativePaths: true
    additionalFiles:
      - xs-app.json

Add the framework section

Without this, the buildThemes task fails because the library's .less files import from sap.ui.core themes, which must be available as a build dependency. Copy the framework section from ui5.yaml as-is.

framework:
  name: SAPUI5
  version: 1.146.0
  libraries:
    - name: sap.ui.core
    - name: themelib_sap_fiori_3

2.2 xs-app.json

Create this file based on the consuming application's xs-app.json. Since a library does not have an index.html or a logout page, remove the ^/index.html$ and ^/logout-page.html$ routes. The final result should look like this:

{
  "welcomeFile": "/index.html",
  "authenticationMethod": "route",
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/resources/(.*)$",
      "target": "/resources/$1",
      "authenticationType": "none",
      "destination": "ui5"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/test-resources/(.*)$",
      "target": "/test-resources/$1",
      "authenticationType": "none",
      "destination": "ui5"
    },
    {
      "source": "^(.*)$",
      "target": "$1",
      "service": "app-front",
      "authenticationType": "ias"
    }
  ]
}

2.3 xs-security.json

Create this file based on the consuming application's xs-security.json. Update xsappname to a value that identifies the library. In this example, the namespace and library module name are concatenated (commyorgreuselib).

{
  "xsappname": "commyorgreuselib",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "description": "Security profile of called application",
  "scopes": [],
  "role-templates": []
}

2.4 mta.yaml

Create this file based on the consuming application's mta.yaml. Replace all occurrences of the application's ID with the library's ID (commyorgreuselib). The html5 module build command remains unchanged as npm install && npm run build:cf.

2.5 package.json

Add the following scripts to build and deploy the library, along with the dependencies they require:

"scripts": {
+  "build:cf": "ui5 build --clean-dest --config ui5-deploy.yaml --dest dist",
+  "build:mta": "rimraf resources mta_archives && mbt build",
-  "deploy": "fiori verify",
+  "deploy": "fiori cfDeploy",
 },
 "devDependencies": {
+  "ui5-task-zipper": "^3.6.0",
+  "mbt": "^1.2.49",
+  "rimraf": "^6.1.3"
 }

Note that the initial deploy script generated by the library template is fiori verify, which only validates the project configuration and does not actually deploy. Replace it with fiori cfDeploy.

3. Deploy and Verify

Run the following commands in both the library and the consuming application projects. Deploy the library first, then the consuming application.

npm run build:mta
npm run deploy

After deploying, verify that both applications are registered in Application Frontend Service:

afctl list

Open the consuming application in the browser and confirm that the dialog from the library opens correctly when the button is pressed.

Figure: Consuming application running on Application Frontend Service, showing the dialog provided by the separately deployed library.

4. Simplifying Library Deployment Configuration with a Yeoman Generator

As described in the previous sections, setting up the deployment configuration for a UI5 library requires creating and updating several files manually. To simplify this process, I created a Yeoman generator (with the help of Claude Code) that automates these steps.

Installation

npm install -g yo generator-ui5-library-af-deploy

Usage

Run the generator in the root directory of the library project:

cd path/to/your-ui5-library
yo ui5-library-af-deploy

The generator reads ui5.yaml and the src/ directory structure to detect the library namespace automatically. If metadata.name in ui5.yaml does not match the detected namespace, the generator asks whether to update it — answering yes is recommended, as the full namespace format is used as a common identifier across the generated files.

After running the generator, install the dependencies and deploy:

npm install
npm run build:mta
npm run deploy

Limitations

  • Targets Application Frontend Service only (not HTML5 Application Repository)
  • Authentication type is fixed to ias
  • Always creates new service instances. If you want to reuse existing instances, adjust mta.yaml after running the generator.

Conclusion

Application Frontend Service simplifies the consumption of UI5 libraries significantly compared to HTML5 Application Repository. Consuming applications only need a single route in xs-app.json to reference a separately deployed library, with no destination configuration required. The library version can also be specified per application, giving each consuming application independent control over which version it uses.

The main remaining challenge is the library deployment configuration itself, as the standard tooling does not generate it for library projects. The Yeoman generator introduced in this blog fills that gap by automating the creation of the necessary files.

The sample code is available at: https://github.com/miyasuta/fiori-use-library-appfrontend

entry.title: Integration Suite migrations are not hard because of the technology, but ...
entry.content:
The technical migration itself is honestly the easiest part most of the time. The real challenge in Integration Suite migrations is everything around coordination, communication, dependencies, stakeholder alignment, and managing expectations.

Yes, integration developers can usually work through technical blockers. There’s already a solid amount of documentation, guides, and community knowledge available to help solve those. But where projects truly struggle is in the non-technical execution layer.

Where Things Actually Break Down?

From experience, the biggest pain points are not in development, but in orchestration:

  • Coordinating with third parties
  • Asking the right migration and modernization questions
  • Managing endpoint switch discussions
  • Convincing clients to move away from legacy patterns
  • Handling RACI / RAID properly
  • Aligning cutover timelines across systems
  • Tracking cross-team dependencies
  • Managing parallel S/4HANA changes alongside integration migration

These are the areas where delays quietly accumulate and often go unnoticed until they become critical.

The Hidden Trap: “No Code Change Required”.

One of the most underestimated risks in these migrations is the assumption:

“There’s no code change required from third parties, so this should be straightforward.”

In reality, this creates ambiguity and hidden gaps:

  • Ownership becomes unclear
  • Communication is minimized or deprioritized
  • Validation cycles are delayed
  • Endpoint changes get underestimated
  • Dependencies remain undocumented

And that’s exactly how migration timelines start slipping, without obvious technical blockers.

The Real Skill Gap!

Most integration developers can figure out:

One of the most underestimated risks in these migrations is the assumption:

✅iFlows  ✅ Mappings  ✅ Adapters ✅ Connectivity

But many struggle with:

❌Stakeholder management ❌ Cross-system coordination ❌ Migration governance ❌ Dependency tracking ❌ Structured planning

This gap is rarely addressed, yet it’s the single biggest driver of project risk.

What’s Missing Today?

There’s a clear need for structured, practical migration playbooks that go beyond just technical steps. Not more documentation on how to build an iFlow, but real-world guidance on how to run a migration successfully.

 

What a Good Migration Playbook Should Cover?

1. Pre-Migration Preparation

  • What an Integration Suite developer should assess upfront
  • Inventory of artifacts and landscape dependencies
  • Interface criticality and usage patterns
  • Business impact assessment

2. Minimum PO/ non-SAP Middleware Knowledge Required

Not every Integration Suite developer needs deep PO/ non-SAP Middleware expertise, but they must understand:

  • How to access and interpret existing PO/ non-SAP Middleware objects
  • Interface types, mappings, and adapter configurations
  • Scheduling, triggers, payloads and dependencies

 

3. Stakeholder Mapping

  • Who owns the interface? (Business vs Technical)
  • Who owns endpoints?
  • Who validates?
  • Who signs off?

Without this clarity, everything slows down.

4. Key Coordination Checkpoints

  • Design validation
  • Endpoint confirmation
  • Connectivity readiness
  • Test cycle alignment
  • Cutover readiness

These should be planned, not discovered mid-project.

5. Migration & Cutover Strategy

  • Big bang vs phased approach
  • Parallel run considerations
  • Rollback planning
  • Freeze windows and deployment coordination

6. Modernization Opportunities

Migration should not be a lift-and-shift exercise.

This is the moment to:

  • Eliminate legacy patterns
  • Simplify integrations
  • Standardize error handling
  • Improve monitoring and observability

7. Transport & Release Dependencies

  • Cross-system release alignment
  • Dependency tracking across teams
  • Change management workflows

A missed dependency can delay an entire cutover.

8. Governance During Execution

  • Clear RACI model (this one is my favourite)
  • RAID (Risks, Assumptions, Issues, Dependencies) tracking
  • Regular sync points
  • Escalation paths

Governance is what keeps everything moving when complexity increases.

Final thoughts!

PO → Integration Suite migrations don’t fail because the technology is too complex. They fail because:

  • Communication is unclear
  • Ownership is ambiguous
  • Dependencies are underestimated
  • Planning is incomplete

The real challenge is orchestration, not implementation.

If we invest more in:

  • Structured migration approaches
  • Clear coordination models
  • Practical playbooks

We can significantly improve delivery success, without needing to reinvent the technical wheel.

Because in the end, it’s not about whether you can migrate— it’s about whether you can migrate smoothly, predictably, and successfully.

entry.title: Re: Pros and Cons of implementing full MDG within Central...
entry.content: I would recommend having MDG as a HUB in its own installation considering multiple ERP sources and other roadmaps. CFIN does not need all the Data elements from MDG data models and quite often its lean for master data reference. MDG can replicate data with SAP's standard SOA and or Idoc direct point to point(P2P) connections without the need for any middleware. MDG as HUB helps expand the Data Model and also manage extedned datamodels. Key mappings is replicated via standard SOA and is all proven methodology.

entry.title: Re: "Entre the MRP Type" error while replication of mater...
entry.content:
Pradeep, 

Clearly the error is coming from your target S4 client. Check Plant‑Specific MRP Group Settings. SPRO → Production → Material Requirements Planning → Master Data → Define MRP Groups

For the receiving plant, check:

  • MRP Type default

  • Whether MRP Type is mandatory for that MRP Group

  • Whether the MRP Group is assigned automatically to the plant

If the plant has an MRP Group with mandatory fields, S/4 will reject materials without MRP Type, even if MDG doesn’t maintain MRP views.

entry.title: I want to know how to resolve real-time revenue errors.
entry.content:
An error occurs stating that real-time posting is not supported when assigning accounts to context assets. I would like to know how to resolve this. 

While reprocessing with real-time revenue recognition allows revenue recognition to run, this error appears every month, so I want to resolve it.

Work procedure

1.Register client projects based on their progress. 

2. Register the account category while linking it to the customer project.

3.Register fixed assets

4.Register a supplier invoice.

5.In the supplier invoice approval workflow, the approver edits the details of the supplier invoice. The edit involves setting fixed assets to the details of the supplier invoice for the order registered in the project's account assignment.

6.Complete the supplier invoice approval flow.

7.Perform revenue recognition

entry.title: Program /SAPAPO/UPDATE_GEOCODE_LOC not exist - 3290708
entry.content:
Program /SAPAPO/UPDATE_GEOCODE_LOC not available to perform this following task as suggested in OSS note DIGITS, please check and suggest.
Client XXX: Geographical data for locations will be affected. See note DIGITS: This is shown when you run the Simplification Item check through SUM framework in non-detailed mode. If you get this message, you have to run the Report  /SAPAPO/UPDATE_GEOCODE_LOC in the particular client mentioned in the message after the upgrade. This report will fix the geo-coordinates of all the affected locations.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Program /SAPAPO/UPDATE_GEOCODE_LOC not exist - 3290708
entry.content:
Dear customer,

Please check blog https://blogs.sap.com/2018/03/26/sap-s4hana-simplification-item-check-how-to-do-it-right./. I'd like to highlight following:

  • Yellow warning messages (return code 4)
  • These you should be aware of and read the corresponding SAP note, but they are no show stoppers for the system conversion/upgrade. Therefore no mandatory action is required to solve these before the conversion. Though there might be activities required after the conversion, or activities where you can decide whether you do them before or after the conversion.

So this message will not stop the upgrade and no need to deal with. Please proceed.

Best regards,
Sylas

entry.title: Data Migration Reimagined – Key Takeaways from SAP Sapphire 2026
entry.content:
🌟 Introduction

Data migration has always been the most critical—and risky—phase of any SAP transformation. But at SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP made it clear: migration is no longer just a technical activity—it is being transformed into an AI-driven, automated, and intelligent process.

With the push toward the Autonomous Enterprise, SAP is redefining how organizations move from legacy systems (ECC or non-SAP) to SAP S/4HANA Cloud—making migration faster, smarter, and less resource-intensive.

🔥 Why Data Migration Is Back in the Spotlight

With the SAP ECC 2027 deadline approaching, organizations are accelerating their move to S/4HANA.
At Sapphire 2026

  • Migration is the gateway to AI adoption
  • Clean, trusted data is essential for Business AI and Joule
  • Cloud transformation (RISE with SAP) depends on efficient data movement

💡 Key message:
👉 No clean data → No effective AI → No intelligent enterprise

🧠 Major Sapphire 2026 Innovations in Data Migration

1. 🤖 AI-Powered Migration Tooling (Game Changer)

SAP introduced agent-led migration tools powered by AI:

  • Automate system analysis
  • Identify and remediate custom code
  • Auto-configure S/4HANA settings
  • Accelerate testing and validation

📊 SAP claims:

  • 35–50% reduction in migration effort 
    • Reference links [linkedin.com], [community.sap.com]

✅ Impact:

  • Faster project timelines
  • Reduced dependency on large migration teams
  • Lower cost of transformation

2. ⚡ Joule-Powered Migration Intelligence

SAP Joule is no longer just a copilot—it’s becoming a migration assistant:

  • Explains legacy code logic automatically
  • Analyzes ATC findings and simplification items
  • Assists in data mapping and transformation
  • Supports decision-making during migration projects

💡 Joule helps teams:

  • Reduce manual analysis effort
  • Improve migration accuracy
  • Speed up complex ECC → S/4 conversions [community.sap.com]

3. ☁️ RISE with SAP as the Migration Backbone

SAP positioned RISE with SAP as the primary path for transformation:

  • Bundles infrastructure, tools, and services
  • Integrates AI-based migration agents
  • Standardizes cloud adoption

👉 Migration is no longer a standalone project—it’s part of a business transformation journey.

4. 🧹 Clean Core Strategy Driving Data Migration

A dominant theme at Sapphire:

  • Remove legacy complexity
  • Standardize business processes
  • Minimize custom code

✅ Benefits:

  • Easier upgrades
  • Faster AI adoption
  • Reduced technical debt

Real-world insights from Sapphire show that:

  • Organizations adopting clean core can deploy AI faster
  • Poor legacy data and customization slow down migration success

5. 📊 Business Data Cloud & Data Readiness

SAP highlighted the growing importance of data platforms:

  • SAP Business Data Cloud unifies enterprise data
  • Provides a foundation for migration + analytics
  • Supports large-scale structured and unstructured data handling

👉 Migration is no longer just ETL—it’s about building a future-ready data ecosystem

🏗️ Evolution of SAP Data Migration Tools

Traditional tools are evolving with intelligence:

Phase Traditional Approach Modern SAP Approach
ExtractManual / batch jobsAutomated discovery with AI
TransformRule-based mappingAI-assisted mapping & cleansing
LoadMigration CockpitSmart orchestration + validation
ValidateManual checksIntelligent anomaly detection

📌 Core tools still include:

  • Migration Cockpit
  • Syniti
  • SAP Data Services
  • SLT / SDI

But now enhanced with automation and AI capabilities

⚠️ Key Challenges Still Remain

Despite innovation, migration isn’t “push-button” yet:

1. Data Quality Issues

  • Duplicate master data
  • Inconsistent formats
  • Missing historical data

💡 Data cleansing often consumes 60% of migration effort in real projects

2. Legacy Complexity

  • Heavy custom ABAP code
  • Poor documentation
  • Multiple disconnected systems

3. Change Management

  • Business process redesign
  • User adoption challenges
  • Skill gaps in cloud + AI

✅ Best Practices for Modern Data Migration

Based on Sapphire insights:

🔹 1. Start with Data Readiness

  • Cleanse and harmonize master data
  • Eliminate duplicates early

🔹 2. Adopt a Phased Migration Strategy

  • Greenfield / Brownfield / Selective approach
  • Avoid “big bang” transformation

🔹 3. Embrace Clean Core

  • Standardize processes
  • Move custom logic to BTP

🔹 4. Use AI Smartly

  • Leverage Joule for analysis
  • Automate repetitive migration tasks

🔹 5. Align Migration with Business Value

  • Prioritize high-impact business processes
  • Link migration to transformation goals

🔮 Future Outlook: Autonomous Data Migration?

SAP’s vision points toward:

  • Self-healing migration processes
  • AI agents managing end-to-end transformation
  • Real-time data validation and correction
  • Continuous migration (not one-time projects)

👉 Migration is evolving from a project into an ongoing capability

🎯 Conclusion

At SAP Sapphire 2026, one message stood out clearly:

👉 Data migration is no longer just about moving data—it’s about enabling intelligence.

With:

  • AI-powered migration tools
  • Joule-driven insights
  • Clean core strategies
  • Cloud-first transformation

SAP is turning migration into a strategic accelerator rather than a bottleneck.

💬 Are you preparing for S/4HANA migration? How are you handling data quality and AI adoption in your projects? Let’s discuss!

 

Thanks,

Venkatesh Golla

entry.title: Building a Cost Center Hierarchy Analytical Model in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Using 0COSTCENTER_0101_HIER and Hierarchy with Directory

 

End-to-end Datasphere modelling flow for the cost center hierarchy scenario.

1. Introduction

This blog explains how the SAP Business Content DataSource 0COSTCENTER_0101_HIER can be replicated into SAP Datasphere and converted from a flat hierarchy extract into a consumable analytical hierarchy. The same pattern can be used when transaction data contains Cost Center as a key dimension and business users need to analyze measures by hierarchy nodes, groups, and leaf-level cost centers.

The purpose of this write-up is to document a practical proof of concept and make the modelling approach easier to repeat. The focus is not only on the individual artifacts, but also on the sequence: source activation, replication, flat-table analysis, hierarchy modelling, dimension modelling, and final analytical consumption.

2. Target Architecture and Modelling Sequence

The complete solution can be organized into six logical layers:

  • Source layer: Activate the standard hierarchy DataSource 0COSTCENTER_0101_HIER in the SAP source system.
  • Replication layer: Replicate the hierarchy through a Datasphere Replication Flow using the SAPI-based connector.
  • Raw hierarchy layer: Analyze the replicated local table, especially the columns that represent parent-child relationships and node types.
  • Hierarchy layer: Create a hierarchy directory dimension and a Hierarchy with Directory view.
  • Master data layer: Create the Cost Center text and Cost Center dimension views, then associate the hierarchy and text views.
  • Consumption layer: Join the Cost Center dimension to the fact model and consume it in an Analytical Model with hierarchy selection.

3. Prerequisites

  • SAP source system access to activate and test the Business Content DataSource 0COSTCENTER_0101_HIER, typically using RSA5/RSA6 and ODQMON.
  • SAP Datasphere space with a configured SAPI-based source connection and permission to create Replication Flows, local tables, SQL views, graphical views, dimensions, hierarchies, and analytical models.
  • A Cost Center attribute/text source or equivalent master data extract to build the Cost Center Dimension and Cost Center Text View.
  • A transaction/fact model that contains Cost Center, and where required, Controlling Area, so that the hierarchy can be consumed against measures.

4. Activate and Validate the SAP Source DataSource

Start by installing the Business Content DataSource 0COSTCENTER_0101_HIER from RSA5 and activating it. In the source system, the hierarchy appears in a tree format; this is useful for functional validation because it confirms that the hierarchy exists before it is replicated to Datasphere.

 

Source-system view of the Cost Center hierarchy after Business Content activation.

After activation, use ODQMON to confirm that the ODP queue is available and that extraction requests are technically successful. This check is important before troubleshooting any Datasphere modelling issue, because it separates source-extraction issues from modelling issues.

 

ODQMON confirmation for the hierarchy extraction request.

5. Replicate the Hierarchy into SAP Datasphere

Create a Replication Flow in Datasphere for 0COSTCENTER_0101_HIER. The output of this step is a local table in Datasphere. At this point, the data is intentionally flat. Datasphere will not automatically show the BW-style hierarchy tree until the hierarchy semantics are defined in modelling artifacts.

 

Replication Flow from the SAP source system to the Datasphere local table.

6. Analyze the Flat Hierarchy Table

This is the most important design step. The replicated table contains all hierarchy records in a flat structure. Before creating the hierarchy artifact, identify the fields that represent the hierarchy name, parent node, child node, node type, node text, and leaf cost center.

Topic

Recommendation / Explanation

HIENM

Hierarchy name or hierarchy identifier. This is used to distinguish one hierarchy from another and to associate the hierarchy records with the directory view.

PARENTID

Parent identifier. It defines where each node sits below another node. Root-level records normally have a blank or initial parent reference.

NODEID / CHILDID

Unique internal identifier of the hierarchy node. Use the actual child-node identifier available in your replicated structure; in this POC, NODEID/CHILDID represents the child side of the relationship.

FIELDNM

Node type discriminator. It tells Datasphere whether a row is a hierarchy group node or a cost center leaf node.

NODENAME

Hierarchy group node value. These rows represent structure/group nodes, not transactional cost centers.

TXTLG

Long text for hierarchy group nodes. This becomes the user-facing label for structural nodes.

KOKRS

Controlling Area. This is required when Cost Center is compounded with Controlling Area.

KOSTL

Cost Center. These are the data-bearing leaf nodes used to join the hierarchy to transaction data.

 

Local hierarchy table showing the parent identifier used to reconstruct the tree.

 

Local hierarchy table showing the node identifier / child-side identifier.

 

Node type and text fields used to separate hierarchy nodes from cost center leaf nodes.

How to Interpret the Node Types

In the replicated local table, FIELDNM acts as the node type column. In this scenario, two node types are relevant:

  • NODENAME: Represents structural hierarchy nodes such as the standard hierarchy, administration, executive, or other grouping nodes. These records usually carry the visible description in TXTLG.
  • KOSTL: Represents actual cost center leaf nodes. These records carry KOKRS and KOSTL and are the records that can be associated with transactional data.

The parent-child relationship is maintained using the parent identifier and the node/child identifier. Datasphere uses these fields to reconstruct the tree in the Hierarchy with Directory artifact.

7. Create the Hierarchy Directory Dimension

Create a SQL view that returns the list of hierarchy root or directory entries. Set the Semantic Usage to Dimension. This view provides the list of available hierarchy roots that users can select from in the analytical experience.

 

Hierarchy directory dimension view with Semantic Usage set to Dimension.

The hierarchy directory view should expose the hierarchy identifier and the descriptive text that users will see when selecting a hierarchy. Keep this artifact simple and stable because the Hierarchy with Directory view depends on it.

8. Create the Cost Center Hierarchy with Directory

Create the hierarchy view and set Semantic Usage to Hierarchy with Directory. Associate this view with the hierarchy directory dimension created in the previous step. This artifact is responsible for converting the flat replicated table into a hierarchy that Datasphere can consume.

 

Hierarchy with Directory view associated with the hierarchy directory dimension.

Attribute Definition and Compounding

If Cost Center is compounded with Controlling Area, define the key columns accordingly and set the representative key to Cost Center. This allows the analytical model to display Cost Center as the primary business value while still preserving the full technical uniqueness required by the source system.

 

Compound key definition with Cost Center as the representative key.

Multiple Hierarchy Settings

In the Multiple Hierarchy Settings dialog, map the flat-table columns into the hierarchy semantics. The exact field names may vary by source, but the design principle remains the same:

  • Map the parent field to the column that stores the parent node identifier, such as PARENTID.
  • Map the child field to the column that stores the current node identifier, such as NODEID or CHILDID, depending on your local table structure.
  • Select the hierarchy directory association and link it to the directory dimension.
  • Use FIELDNM as the node type column.
  • Define NODENAME as a structural node type and map its display text to TXTLG.
  • Define KOSTL as the data-node type and map its key columns to KOKRS and KOSTL.

 

Multiple Hierarchy Settings for parent, child, hierarchy directory, node type, and NODENAME text.

 

Data-node configuration for KOSTL using KOKRS and KOSTL.

9. Create the Cost Center Text View

Create a text view from the relevant Cost Center text source and set Semantic Usage to Text. This view provides readable descriptions for Cost Center and, when applicable, Controlling Area. Without a text view, the hierarchy may still work technically, but the analytical output will be less user-friendly.

 

Cost Center Text View with Semantic Usage set to Text.

10. Create the Cost Center Dimension

Create the Cost Center Dimension using the relevant Cost Center attribute source. Set Semantic Usage to Dimension. Then associate the Cost Center Dimension with both the Cost Center Hierarchy with Directory and the Cost Center Text View.

 

Cost Center Dimension associated with the hierarchy and text views.

This dimension becomes the reusable semantic object that can be consumed by transaction models. The association to the hierarchy enables hierarchical display, while the association to the text view improves readability for end users.

11. Add the Cost Center Dimension to the Fact / Analytical Model

Add the Cost Center Dimension to the transaction model where Cost Center exists in the fact data. The fact model should expose business measures and include the key fields needed to associate with the Cost Center Dimension. If Cost Center is compounded, make sure the join condition includes the required compounding field, such as Controlling Area.

 

Analytical Model consuming the transaction fact source and the Cost Center Dimension.

 

Final graphical flow showing the hierarchy and transaction modelling chain.

12. Consume the Hierarchy in the Analytical Preview

Run the analytical model and place Cost Center in the row axis. From the Cost Center dimension options, choose the hierarchy presentation and select the required hierarchy root. Datasphere then renders the Cost Center values in hierarchical form.

 

Selecting the hierarchy presentation for Cost Center in the analytical preview.

 

Selecting the required hierarchy root during analytical consumption.

 

Final hierarchical output in the analytical preview.

13. Validation Checklist

  • Confirm that the hierarchy root list appears from the hierarchy directory dimension.
  • Confirm that structural nodes are displayed with readable descriptions from the NODENAME/TXTLG mapping.
  • Confirm that cost center leaf nodes appear below the correct parent nodes.
  • Confirm that measures roll up correctly from leaf cost centers to parent hierarchy nodes.
  • Confirm that the Cost Center Dimension join to the fact data includes all required key fields, especially Controlling Area if Cost Center is compounded.
  • Compare a small hierarchy branch with the source-system hierarchy to confirm the parent-child reconstruction.

14. Common Issues and Practical Tips

Topic

Recommendation / Explanation

Hierarchy appears flat

Check whether the view is set to Hierarchy with Directory and whether parent, child, hierarchy directory, and node type mappings are correctly assigned.

Root hierarchy is not selectable

Validate the hierarchy directory dimension and its association to the hierarchy view.

Cost centers appear without text

Check the Cost Center Text View and its association to the Cost Center Dimension.

Measures do not roll up correctly

Validate that cost center leaf nodes are defined as data nodes and that the dimension is joined correctly to the fact model.

Duplicate or missing leaf nodes

Review the compounded key definition. For Cost Center, include Controlling Area where required and use Cost Center as the representative key.

Source and Datasphere hierarchy do not match

Compare the parent-child IDs in the local table against the source hierarchy branch and confirm the selected hierarchy root.

15. Conclusion

The key learning from this exercise is that hierarchy replication and hierarchy consumption are two different steps. The 0COSTCENTER_0101_HIER DataSource brings the hierarchy structure into Datasphere as a flat local table. The hierarchy becomes analytically useful only after the parent-child fields, node type column, hierarchy directory, text mapping, and cost center dimension associations are modelled correctly.

Once the semantic layer is in place, the Cost Center hierarchy can be reused across analytical models. This approach helps SAP Datasphere provide a business-friendly hierarchy experience similar to what users expect from BW, while keeping the modelling transparent and extensible for future hierarchy scenarios.

Note: Object names and screenshots are from a proof-of-concept scenario. Adjust field names and associations based on your actual Datasphere local table structure and source-system configuration.

entry.title: Re: BAPI_GOODSMVT_CREATE - Error Combination of movement...
entry.content:
In release 618, the movement type 543 O is expected to be used with GM_CODE 06 (Subcontracting), not GM_CODE 03. Since your PO is deleted, the system cannot reconcile the subcontracting consumption, hence the error. In 606, the system was more lenient, allowing GM_CODE 03 with 543 even without a PO reference.

Recommended Next Step

  • Either reconfigure movement type 543 in customizing to allow GM_CODE 03 again, or
  • Switch your BAPI call to GM_CODE 06 if you want to stay aligned with SAP’s standard subcontracting process.

entry.title: Re: Has anyone replicated CKM3N logic using direct SAP da...
entry.content:
To replicate CKM3N logic outside SAP, you need to work with Material Ledger (ML) totals and document tables, not just MLDOC. The transaction builds its ALV tree output from a combination of ML header, totals, and cost component tables, applying price determination logic (single-level/multilevel) to calculate actual costs and variances. SAP provides reporting tables (FCML) and drilldown transactions (KKML0) that are much easier to consume in BI/DW than reverse-engineering CKM3N’s ABAP.

Having said that, if your BI/DW requirement is mainly reporting, I recommend activating FCML tables* and consume them directly. If you need exact CKM3N replication, you will have to rebuild the Price Determination Structure logic using CKML totals + MLDOC, but that is far more complex.

Not having the exact solution for ABAP CDS view, I used the following workaround (groupping two fields into one column) (Bharathi Sakthivel: 'Workaround for dynamic navigation in CDS with ABAP'):

entry.title: Fiori tile showing "Target Not Found" error on dynamic tile count — S/4HANA
entry.content:
How to fix the “Target Not Found” error on a Fiori dynamic tile count

Your tile opens fine — but the count is broken. Here’s exactly why it happens and two ways to fix it permanently.


You open the SAP Fiori Launchpad and notice one of your dynamic tiles is showing an error badge on its count — but when you click it, the app loads perfectly. The tile works. The number doesn’t. What’s going on?

This is a surprisingly common issue, and the root cause is almost always the same: the tile’s Dynamic Data Service URL is pointing to the wrong OData service or entity set — one that belongs to a different app entirely.

The tile navigates correctly because its target URL is fine. The count fails because its data service URL is mapped to someone else’s OData.
The scenario

In this example, a “Manage Purchase Orders” tile was misconfigured to fetch its count from MM_PUR_POITEMS_MONI_SRV — which is the OData service for Monitor Purchase Order Items, a completely different app. The network tab confirms it: a $count request to that service returns an error, while the tile’s navigation itself works fine.

Two ways to fix it

Depending on your situation, you have two approaches. If the current OData service is simply misconfigured, fix the URL directly (Option 1). If it’s a deprecated service that SAP no longer pushes updates to, create a fresh dynamic tile with the correct service from scratch (Option 2).

Option 1 :

Step-by-step fix
1 : Capture the failing OData service. Open browser DevTools → Network tab. Click the broken tile. Find the request with $count in the URL that’s returning an error. Copy the OData service name from the request URI — for example, MM_PUR_POITEMS_MONI_SRV.

2: Identify what app that service belongs to. Go to the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library → All Apps. Paste the service name in the search bar. This will tell you which app actually owns that OData service — and confirm it’s not the same app as your tile.

3: Find the tile in the Fiori Launchpad. In the Fiori Launchpad, go to Settings → Groups. Enter the group name where your tile lives, locate the tile, and note its catalog assignment.

4: Open the tile configuration and inspect the Dynamic Data Service URL. In the catalog editor, open the tile. Under Dynamic Data Service URL, you’ll see it pointing to the wrong OData service — that’s the culprit.

 

5: Get the correct OData service from the Fiori App Library. Search for the tile’s actual app name (e.g. “Manage Purchase Orders”) in the Fiori App Library. Under the Configuration section, copy the correct OData service name.

 

6: Verify the service is active in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE. Go to T-code /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE, click Filter, enter the correct OData service name, and confirm the status shows Active.
Important: If the service is not active, activate it before proceeding. An inactive service will continue to break the count regardless of any other changes you make.

 

7: Find the correct entity set via SAP Gateway Client. From within /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE, select the active service and open the SAP Gateway Client. Browse Entity Sets and find the one that should be used for the count. Copy its full service URI.

 

8: Update the tile’s Dynamic Data Service URL and save. Return to the tile configuration in the catalog. Replace the incorrect service URL with the correct one from the previous step. Save.

 

9: Hard refresh and verify. Press Ctrl + Shift + R (or Cmd + Shift + R on Mac) to hard refresh the Fiori Launchpad. The tile count should now display correctly.

Option 2 :

If the current tile’s OData service is deprecated, fixing the URL is only a partial solution — SAP will not push future updates to deprecated services. The cleaner fix is to retire the old tile and create a new dynamic tile with the correct, current service from the start.

1.Copy configuration from the existing static tile. Open the existing static Manage Purchase Order tile in the catalog and note down all key details: title, subtitle, icon, semantic object, action, and navigation target.

2.Create a new dynamic tile in the catalog. In the Fiori Launchpad catalog, add a new tile and choose the Dynamic tile type. Paste in the configuration values from the static tile.

3.Set the correct Dynamic Data Service URL. In the Dynamic Data Service URL field, enter the correct, active OData service URI — the one identified from the Fiori App Library and verified in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE  This is the only field that differs from the original tile.

 

4.Add the new tile to the group. Go to Settings → Groups in the Fiori Launchpad. Add the newly created dynamic tile to the appropriate group. Remove or hide the old broken tile.

5.Verify in the Fiori Launchpad. Hard refresh the launchpad. The new dynamic tile should display its count correctly without any errors.

When to choose Option 2: If the tile's original service is flagged as deprecated in the Fiori App Library or IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE, go with this approach — it keeps your launchpad aligned with SAP's current service landscape.

Why does this happen?

This misconfiguration typically occurs after a tile is copied or cloned in the launchpad catalog without updating its Dynamic Data Service URL. The navigation target gets updated (so the app opens correctly) but the count service URL is left pointing at whatever the source tile used — often a completely different OData service. It can also happen during system migrations or when business roles are transported across landscapes without a proper review of tile-level configuration details.

Conclusion

Fiori dynamic tile count errors are easy to overlook — the tile itself keeps working, so it can feel like a cosmetic issue. But it's a service misconfiguration with a clear fix once you know where to look.

The Dynamic Data Service URL on your tile must point to the correct, active OData service for that specific app — not any other app in the same catalog or group. If the service is simply wrong, fix it directly (Option 1). If it's deprecated and SAP no longer pushes updates to it, build a fresh dynamic tile with the right service (Option 2).

A quick check in the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library and /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE is all it takes to restore the count and keep your Fiori Launchpad accurate for your end users.

entry.content:
Hello everyone,

Greetings!

I'm facing an issue while attempting the SAP certification exam C_TS452_2601. 

Initially, I scheduled the exam on 25th May at 4:30 PM IST. However, during the launch of the exam, the system displayed a "System temporarily unavailable" message continuously.

Even after refreshing for nearly an hour, the issue was not resolved. I then created a support case, but there was no response and eventually the exam slot got cancelled.

I rescheduled the exam again for today (26th May) at 10:30 AM IST, but unfortunately the exact same issue is happening again. I have been refreshing continuously for more than 20 mins, and the exam is still not launching. 

I also updated the existing support case with an additional comment mentioning that the issue persists.

Has anyone else faced a similar issue recently with SAP certification exams? if yes, how was it resolved? Any guidance or suggestions would be really helpful.

Thank you in advance.

Regards,

VM

entry.title: Guidance Needed: LMS → Snowflake Data Ingestion & Identifying Correct API Endpoints
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

We are currently working on a project to ingest SAP SuccessFactors LMS data into Snowflake for reporting and analytics. We’ve reviewed the available documentation, including the Account Data report reference here:

  1.  https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/lm/3354618614.html
  2. https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-learning/sap-successfactors-learning-odata-apis/background-knowledge-for-sap-successfactors-learning-web-services
  3. https://me.sap.com/notes

    At this stage, we are able to retrieve the Student table, but we are struggling to identify the correct API endpoints for additional user‑related fields. Many attributes we need (e.g., extended user profile fields, account‑level metadata, etc.) do not appear in the Student table or in the standard PRD/BIRT report definitions.

    Our questions are:

    1. Which LMS API endpoints expose full user information beyond the Student table?

    2. Is there a recommended approach or best practice for extracting complete user data for external ingestion (e.g., Snowflake, data lake, etc.)?

    3. Has anyone successfully implemented an LMS → Snowflake or similar pipeline and can share which endpoints or reports were required?

    4. Are there hidden or less obvious endpoints for user attributes that are not documented in the standard help pages?

    Any guidance, examples, or direction to the correct documentation would be greatly appreciated.

    Thank you!

entry.title: Disabling CSRF Token Validation in SAP for Testing with Postman and External Applications
entry.content:
Introduction

When working with SAP OData services, developers frequently encounter CSRF token validation errors while testing APIs using tools like Postman or integrating with third-party applications. These errors commonly occur during POST, PUT, PATCH, or DELETE operations because SAP Gateway enforces CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) protection for security purposes.

In this blog, we will walk through:

  • What CSRF token validation is in SAP
  • Why Postman and external applications face CSRF-related issues
  • How to disable CSRF token validation for a specific SAP OData service
  • Testing the service using SAP Gateway Client and Postman

What is CSRF Token Validation in SAP?

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) protection is a security mechanism implemented by SAP Gateway to prevent unauthorized requests from malicious applications.

For modifying operations such as:

  • POST
  • PUT
  • PATCH
  • DELETE

SAP requires a valid X-CSRF-Token header.

If the token is missing or invalid, SAP returns errors such as:

  • 403 Forbidden
  • CSRF token validation failed

This issue is very common while testing APIs from:

  • Postman
  • External middleware applications
  • Custom UI applications
  • Third-party integrations

Common Issue Faced in Postman

Most developers directly trigger a POST or PUT request from Postman without first fetching the CSRF token.

As a result, SAP Gateway rejects the request due to missing token validation.

Go to transaction code: SICF

SICF ->service->GUI Configuration

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Setting the value to 0 disables CSRF token validation for the service

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Test the Service in SAP Gateway Client:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Testing from Postman

After disabling CSRF validation, you can directly perform:

  • POST requests
  • PUT requests
  • PATCH requests
  • DELETE requests

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

Efficient API testing is essential for successful SAP integrations, and managing CSRF token validation is a key part of that process. Whether you are working with Postman, middleware platforms, or custom applications, knowing how to configure and troubleshoot CSRF settings can save considerable development time. Always ensure that any security relaxation performed for testing is carefully controlled and documented to avoid risks in productive environments.

ABAP Development 

OData 

SAP NetWeaver Application Server for ABAP 

NW ABAP Gateway (OData)
